title: English milk toffee
subtitle: Old-fashioned pulled toffee with butter and condensed milk
excerpt: English milk toffee is the soft, creamy, slightly grainy toffee sold in paper twists at sweet shops; not the hard, brittle kind but the kind that gives a little when you bite it, with a milky caramel flavour that comes from the condensed milk cooking with sugar and butter. It is pulled from the pan between soft ball and firm ball stage, poured into a tin, and cut into squares before it sets completely. The texture is the point: smooth, dense, and just soft enough to chew.
sourceNotes: Milk toffee is a distinctly British confection, associated with the Edwardian and Victorian sweet-shop tradition. It differs from hard toffee (cooked to hard crack stage) and from Scottish tablet (higher sugar, more granular texture, crumbles rather than chews). The use of condensed milk (introduced commercially in Britain in the 1860s) is central to the texture: it adds dairy solids and pre-cooked sugar that produce the characteristic smoothness. Regional variants include Yorkshire toffee and Devon cream toffee.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 40 squares from a 20 × 20 cm tin
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 2 weeks in an airtight tin at room temperature, wrapped in parchment or waxed paper.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; cooked on the hob to firm ball stage (120 to 125 degrees C)

glossaryTerms.definition: A stage in sugar cookery reached at 120 to 125 degrees C (248 to 257 degrees F). Dropped into cold water, the syrup forms a firm ball that holds its shape but flattens when pressed. Firm ball stage is used for milk toffee, fudge, and some caramels. Below this temperature the ball will be too soft; above 130 degrees C (hard ball stage) the candy becomes stiffer and more chewy. A sugar thermometer is the most reliable method.

content.text: (120 to 125 degrees C). Below this it will be too soft and sticky; above 130 degrees C it becomes hard and chewy like a traditional boiled sweet. Stir continuously throughout cooking; condensed milk scorches on the base of the pan if left to settle. The mixture is ready when it is a deep golden-brown and pulls away from the sides of the pan.

Line a 20 × 20 cm tin with lightly buttered parchment. Combine 400 g caster sugar, 397 g condensed milk, 100 g butter, and 2 tbsp golden syrup in a large, heavy-based saucepan. Heat gently over low-medium heat, stirring constantly until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Increase to medium heat and continue stirring throughout; do not walk away. Cook to 120 to 125 degrees C on a sugar thermometer; firm ball stage. The mixture will be a deep golden-brown and will pull away from the sides of the pan. Remove from the heat and stir in 1 tsp vanilla extract. Pour into the prepared tin without scraping the pan. Leave to cool at room temperature for 20 minutes, then score into squares with a sharp knife while still slightly warm. Leave to set completely; about 1 hour. Cut through the scored lines and separate the squares.

items.problem: Toffee is grainy and crystalline rather than smooth
items.solution: The sugar crystallised during cooking. The golden syrup is there to prevent this — make sure it is included. Also avoid stirring the sides of the pan back into the mixture during cooking. If crystals form on the side of the pan, brush them away with a pastry brush dipped in cold water.

items.problem: Toffee is very soft and won't set firm
items.solution: It did not reach firm ball stage. The temperature must reach 120 degrees C minimum. Test with a thermometer rather than judging by colour. A soft result can sometimes be recovered by returning it to the pan and cooking a further few minutes, then re-pouring.

items.problem: Caught on the bottom of the pan
items.solution: The heat was too high or it was not stirred continuously. Condensed milk scorches easily. Use medium rather than high heat and keep the spoon moving at all times, including at the base of the pan.
